1: A direct revelation: "Having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end." The Greek eis telos means both "to the end" (until the last moment) and "completely / to the uttermost." The cross is the telos of the love — the love is not merely persistent but reaches its fullest expression in the death of the Son for those he loves. The Evangelist's opening summary of the farewell section (John 13–17) establishes the cross as the disclosure of the full extent of divine love. The love that sent the Son into the world (John 3:16) now carries him all the way to the cross.
2: A revelation of God: "the devil had already prompted Judas... to betray Jesus." The human betrayal is set against its supernatural context: behind Judas's decision is the adversary's work. The Evangelist does not excuse Judas (13:27 will show him acting after Satan enters him); he frames the betrayal within the cosmic conflict that the cross will resolve (John 12:31). The one who will be "drawn out" (John 12:32) by the lifting up of the Son is already at work through Judas at the table. The final battle is being staged at the Passover meal.
3: A revelation of God: "Jesus knew that the Father had put all things under his power, and that he had come from God and was returning to God." The foot-washing is performed by one who is fully aware of his divine identity, authority, and destination. He does not wash feet from a position of uncertainty or weakness; he washes feet from the position of total sovereignty and perfect knowledge. The Servant who humbles himself (Isaiah 52:13 — "my servant... will be raised and lifted up and highly exalted") acts from the security of knowing who he is.
4: A revelation of God: "so he got up from the meal, took off his outer clothing, and wrapped a towel around his waist." The dressing for service — removing the robe of the guest, wrapping the servant's towel — is the enacted parable of the incarnation itself. The Prologue described the Word taking on flesh; here Jesus acts it out with outer clothing and towel. Philippians 2:7 ("he emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant") is the theological articulation of what John 13:4 depicts. The one who is Lord of all clothes himself as the servant of all.
5: A revelation of God: the washing of feet — the task of the lowest household slave, considered too menial for a Jewish student to perform for his teacher — is performed by the Son of God for his disciples. The Servant of Isaiah 52–53 who makes himself nothing and takes the form of a servant is here embodied in the act of kneeling and washing. The cross will be the ultimate form of this descent; the foot-washing is the sign that precedes it, the enacted prophecy of the death that is coming.
6: A revelation of God: Peter's protest — "Lord, are you going to wash my feet?" — is the appropriate response to the inversion of all social and theological categories. That the Lord should wash the servant is incomprehensible to Peter. His incomprehension is the right starting point; Jesus' answer (v7) will invite him not to understand immediately but to trust that the understanding will come. The resurrection will be the moment when the disciples understand what the foot-washing meant (v7 — "later you will understand").
7: A revelation of God: "You do not realize now what I am doing, but later you will understand." The "later" refers to the post-resurrection understanding the Spirit would give (John 14:26; John 16:13). The pattern is consistent throughout John: the full meaning of Christ's acts is only available in retrospect, illuminated by the resurrection and the Spirit. The foot-washing's full Christological significance — the Lord serving, the death the service anticipates, the cleansing it provides — will become clear when the cross and resurrection have happened.
8: A revelation of God: "Unless I wash you, you have no part with me." The washing is not optional — it is the condition of belonging to Christ. The cleansing Christ performs is not merely physical; it is the cleansing that the cross will accomplish. The one who refuses the Son's service refuses the Son himself. Peter's refusal is well-intentioned reverence; Jesus' answer reveals that the refusal of what Christ does is equivalent to the refusal of Christ. This is the Christological principle: you cannot separate the person from the act of self-giving service.
9: A revelation of God: Peter's overreaction — "not just my feet but my hands and my head as well!" — is the Evangelist's characteristic portrait of the impulsive disciple who moves from resistance to excessive enthusiasm. Jesus' response in v10 will qualify: the cleansing he provides is complete and not divisible into parts. The principle mirrors baptism in the NT: the washing once applied is sufficient; it is not repeated but remembered. The foot-washing at the table is the enacted expression of the once-for-all cleansing the cross will accomplish.
10: A revelation of God: "Those who have had a bath need only to wash their feet; their whole body is clean." The one who belongs to Christ — who has received the complete cleansing — does not need repeated fundamental cleansing but daily renewal from the accumulated defilement of walking in the world. The theological distinction (complete initial cleansing / ongoing renewal) will become the NT pattern: the once-for-all nature of the atonement plus the ongoing intercession of the glorified Christ (1 John 2:1–2).
11: A revelation of God: "not every one of you" — Jesus knows Judas is not clean because he knows who will betray him (John 6:70–71). The divine foreknowledge that the Evangelist has attributed to Jesus throughout (John 2:25; John 4:17–18; John 6:64) operates here. The foot-washing, which is the enacted parable of the cross, is performed even for Judas — the one who will hand Jesus over. The love that washes feet goes to the end, even to the feet of the betrayer. The cross will be offered for those who crucify, as well as for those who follow.
12: A revelation of God: "Do you understand what I have done for you?" Jesus finishes the act and invites interpretation. The question is the same question the resurrection will ask: do you now understand who I am and what I have done? The foot-washing is a Christological test of comprehension — those who understand it understand the incarnation and the cross. Those who understand the cross understand the foot-washing. The two events interpret each other.
13: A revelation of God: "You call me Teacher and Lord, and rightly so, for that is what I am." The acknowledgment of the disciples' right theology is combined with the enactment of what that theology requires: the Lord who washes feet is the Lord who defines lordship by service. The disciples have the correct categories; they have not yet understood the substance those categories describe. The Teacher who has just taught by washing feet, the Lord who has just governed by serving — these are the Christological definitions that the church will take centuries to absorb.
14: A revelation of God: "Now that I, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also should wash one another's feet." The servant-love of the Son becomes the standard for the community of the Son. The one who washes feet commands foot-washing — not as ritual obligation but as the form of life that reflects the character of Christ. The new commandment of v34 is given content here: love one another as the Lord has loved you, which means kneeling for one another as he has knelt for you.
15: A revelation of God: "I have set you an example that you should do as I have done for you." The Greek hypodeigma (example/pattern) connects the foot-washing to the cross: the example Christ sets is not merely a lesson in service but the pattern of life-through-death, self-giving-for-others that the cross will enact in its fullest form. To follow the example is to inhabit the shape of the incarnation — the downward movement that becomes the ground of the upward movement (Philippians 2:5–11).
16: A revelation of God: "no servant is greater than his master, nor is a messenger greater than the one who sent him." The principle of derived authority applied to the disciples' service: if the one who sent them washed feet, the ones sent should expect to wash feet. The same principle will govern the disciples' reception of persecution (John 15:20): they should not expect better treatment than their Lord received. The Christological principle and the missional principle are the same: the servant follows the servant-master's pattern.
17: A revelation of God: "you will be blessed if you do them." The beatitude on doing-what-Christ-does is the Christological application of the OT wisdom principle (Psalms 1:1–3 — blessed is the one who walks in God's way). The blessing attached to following Christ's example is not merit-earning but participation in the life that flows from union with the one who is the resurrection and the life. The blessed life of the disciple is the life shaped by the pattern of Christ.
18: A direct fulfillment: "He who shared my bread has turned against me" — Psalms 41:9 is cited as fulfilled in Judas's betrayal. The Psalm speaks of the one who ate at the psalmist's table lifting his heel against him — the betrayal of table-fellowship, the most intimate social bond in the ancient world. Jesus has just washed Judas's feet and shared bread with him; the intimacy of the last supper is the intimacy of the fulfilled Psalm. The betrayal of Christ by one of the Twelve is not an unforeseen disaster but a scripturally anticipated element of the passion that demonstrates divine foreknowledge.
19: A direct revelation: "I am telling you now before it happens, so that when it does happen you will believe that I am who I am." The egō eimi ("I am") without a predicate — the absolute use — is the divine self-disclosure language of Isaiah 43:10 and Exodus 3:14. Jesus claims divine foreknowledge of the betrayal as evidence of his divine identity. When Judas' betrayal fulfills what Jesus said, the disciples are to understand that the one who knew it in advance is the one who bears the divine name. The prophecy is given to produce faith, not to produce fear.
20: A revelation of God: "whoever accepts anyone I send accepts me; and whoever accepts me accepts the one who sent me." The chain of representation — the Father sends the Son; the Son sends the disciples — means that reception of the messenger is reception of the sender. The Apostolic mission is grounded in this chain: those who receive the apostles' word receive Christ's word; those who receive Christ receive the Father. The missional authority of the church derives from this sent-and-sending relationship that originates in the Trinity.
21: A revelation of God: "Jesus was troubled in spirit and testified, 'Truly, truly, I tell you, one of you is going to betray me.'" The troubled spirit echoes John 11:33 (at Lazarus's tomb) and John 12:27 (approaching the hour of the cross). Each time, genuine anguish is paired with the Son's embrace of what is coming. He is not unaffected by the betrayal; he is troubled. But he is not deterred. The divine foreknowledge includes the emotional weight of what is known: the Son who knows everything, including Judas's intent, is still moved by the cost of it.
22: A theme: the disciples' staring at one another in bewilderment — each wondering if they are the betrayer — is the honest self-examination that Jesus' announcement requires. The question "Is it I, Lord?" (Matthew 26:22) in the Synoptics is the right question for each disciple to ask. The Evangelist's version (looking at one another) captures the corporate dimension: the community of Christ is confronted with the possibility of betrayal within its own ranks, and the response is not accusation of others but uncertain self-examination.
23: A revelation of God: "the disciple whom Jesus loved, was reclining next to him." The Beloved Disciple — consistently unnamed by the Evangelist — appears here for the first time in John. The position of reclining next to Jesus (literally, at his chest — the same language used of the Son in the Father's bosom in John 1:18) is the position of intimate access. The Beloved Disciple functions in John as the witness of supreme proximity to Christ — the one whose testimony the Gospel ultimately rests on (John 21:24).
24: A theme: Peter's gesture — motioning to the Beloved Disciple to ask Jesus — shows the two characteristic disciples in their respective roles. Peter is the one who acts and leads; the Beloved Disciple is the one who hears and understands. The Evangelist places them in complementary positions throughout the passion and resurrection narratives (John 20:3–10; John 21:7). Together they model the two aspects of discipleship: action and contemplation, doing and understanding.
25: A revelation of God: "Leaning back against Jesus, he asked him, 'Lord, who is it?'" The physical intimacy of the scene is the intimacy of the one who knows the Son most closely asking the question that is on every disciple's heart. The Beloved Disciple's closeness to Jesus — which mirrors the Son's closeness to the Father (John 1:18) — gives him access to the answer. The chain of knowledge runs from the Father to the Son (v3) to the one who leans against him. Intimate proximity to Christ is the ground of deeper understanding.
26: A revelation of God: Jesus indicates the betrayer by the act of giving him the piece of bread. The offering of the bread to Judas is an act of grace and identification simultaneously — an extension of fellowship to the one who is about to destroy fellowship. The same hand that washed Judas's feet gives bread to the betrayer. The Evangelist shows the Son extending the final offer of love even as the betrayal is consummated. Judas has been fully known and fully served; what follows is his own choice.
27: A revelation of God: "As soon as Judas took the bread, Satan entered into him." The moment of final decision — the taking of the bread — is the moment of supernatural entry. Judas has been prompted (v2) and now is fully possessed. The cosmic adversary who was "driven out" by the lifting up of the Son (John 12:31) is at this moment entering the betrayer through whom the lifting up will be arranged. The Evangelist presents the passion as both a human and a supernatural event: Judas acts; Satan acts; and God's purpose is accomplished through both.
28: A theme: "no one at the meal understood why Jesus said this to him." The disciples' incomprehension at the moment of the betrayal's arrangement is the Evangelist's consistent portrait of disciples operating below the level of events they are witnessing. Even with the direct indication of v26, no one grasps what is happening. The passion is not something the disciples manage or understand in advance; it overtakes them. The understanding comes only after the resurrection interprets what they witnessed.
29: A theme: the disciples' reasonable misinterpretation — buying food or giving to the poor — reflects how ordinary the moment looks from inside it. The most theologically catastrophic act in the Gospel (the betrayal of the Son of God) is happening while others are thinking about festival logistics. The Evangelist's irony is pointed: the mission of redemption that spans eternity is occurring in real time while human beings think about bread and charity. The grandeur of the event is hidden within its ordinariness.
30: A revelation of God: "As soon as Judas had taken the bread, he went out. And it was night." The Evangelist's three words — "And it was night" — are the most theologically compressed sentence in the passion narrative. Judas goes out into the darkness that the Evangelist has identified with everything that opposes Christ (John 1:5 — the darkness that did not overcome the light; John 3:19 — people loving darkness rather than light). The exit of Judas into the night is the exit of the darkness from the upper room and into the betrayal. Night is the realm of those who do not walk in the light (John 11:10; John 12:35).
31: A direct revelation: "Now the Son of Man is glorified and God is glorified in him." With Judas gone, the passion has effectively begun — and Jesus announces it as glorification, not tragedy. The double glorification — Son of Man glorified; God glorified in him — is the Evangelist's consistent framing of the cross as the mutual disclosure of the Father and the Son. The cross is the moment when both the love of the Father and the obedience of the Son are fully disclosed, and in that disclosure, both are glorified.
32: A revelation of God: "If God is glorified in him, God will glorify the Son in himself, and will glorify him at once." The resurrection and exaltation are the Father's response to the Son's glorification through the cross. The passive verb — "will be glorified" — is the divine passive: God will do this. The Philippians 2:9–11 hymn describes the same logic: the humiliation of the cross is followed by the Father's exaltation of the Son above every name. The "at once" signals the immediacy of the resurrection — the Son is not left in death but raised and glorified in the Father's presence without delay.
33: A revelation of God: "My children, I will be with you only a little longer." The term "children" (teknia) — the only time Jesus uses it of the disciples in John — marks the intimate relationship the farewell discourses will elaborate. The parting is coming; the "little while" before the cross is measured in hours. The one who will be with them "another way" (John 14:16–18 — through the Spirit) is preparing them for his physical departure. The farewell that begins here in v33 will be the most sustained disclosure of Christ's identity and the disciples' future in the entire Gospel.
34: A fulfillment: "A new command I give you: Love one another. As I have loved you, so you must love one another." The newness is not the command to love — Leviticus 19:18 already commanded love of neighbor. The newness is the standard: "as I have loved you." The love of Christ — cruciform, servant-shaped, death-bearing — becomes the norm for the community's love. This is the new covenant's new capacity: Jeremiah 31:33 promised the law written on the heart by the Spirit; the new command here is the Christological content of that law — love shaped by the cross, powered by the Spirit (John 15:10–12).
35: A revelation of God: "By this everyone will know that you are my disciples, if you love one another." The identifying mark of the community of Christ is not doctrinal precision, ritual observance, or ethnic identity but the quality of their love for one another. The love that flows from union with the crucified and risen Son is the distinctive feature of the new community. The watching world is to recognize the disciples of Jesus by the shape of their love — which is the shape of Christ's love for them.
36: A revelation of God: Peter's question — "Lord, where are you going?" — is the question the disciples will ask in various forms throughout the farewell discourses (John 14:5; John 16:5). Jesus answers with the distinction between "now" and "later": he cannot follow now (the cross is not for Peter yet); he will follow later (Peter will die a martyr's death, John 21:18–19). The "where I am going" is both the cross (the immediate destination) and the Father's house (the ultimate destination, John 14:2). Both are real; the disciples will not arrive at the second without going through the first.
37: A revelation of God: Peter's "I will lay down my life for you" is sincere and wrong. He believes it when he says it. The Evangelist includes it because Jesus' response will reverse it: "you will deny me three times." The reversal is not a dismissal of Peter but an exposure of what human devotion can and cannot do. The one who will actually lay down his life — for Peter — is the one speaking. The confession Peter cannot yet make, the loyalty he cannot yet maintain, will become possible only after the resurrection.
38: A revelation of God: "before the rooster crows, you will disown me three times." The prediction of Peter's denial is the final act of the divine foreknowledge that has been operating throughout chapter 13: Jesus knew about the betrayal (v11), knew who the betrayer was (v26), and now knows about the denial. Every human failure in the passion is known in advance by the Son and is incorporated into the plan of the Father. Peter's denial — like Judas's betrayal — is not an obstacle to the mission but a part of the human landscape through which the mission proceeds. The one who is denied three times will be restored three times (John 21:15–17).
